note: 2026 top-3 prospects: college production transcribed from DRAFT_SAVANT_RESEARCH.md; birthdate is public record; KenPom strength-of-schedule rank is a live figure (see each prospect's sos_asof) and should be re-checked before trusting in a later session. No live pull yet otherwise.

players.name: AJ Dybantsa
players.scouting: Led the nation in scoring (25.5 ppg, 894 points - third-most by a freshman in NCAA D-I history) on plus efficiency (.600 TS%) at elite usage, with prototype wing size and a 42" vertical. The flags are defensive translation and shot diet: a modest 1.7% steal rate, thin block rate, and low 3-point volume (24.5% rate) mean the shooting and defensive projection carry more uncertainty than the scoring does.

players.name: Darryn Peterson
players.scouting: An excellent shot-maker (82.6% FT, 38.2% on heavy 3-point volume) with elite turnover economy (10.4% TOV%) and unusually good stocks for a guard (2.9% STL, 2.3% BLK). The swing skill is passing: a 12.5% assist rate is thin for a projected lead guard, and the sample itself is injury-shortened (24 games, missed time with cramping/hamstring/ankle issues) - Swish Theory noted his assist rate climbing over his healthier final two months.

players.name: Cameron Boozer
players.scouting: The class's best analytic profile: elite efficiency at high usage (.653 TS%), the class-best impact number (18.7 BPM Sports-Reference / 20.1 Torvik), strong stocks (3.2% STL, 3.8% BLK), elite turnover economy (8.1% TOV%), and hub-level passing for a big (26.4% AST%). Swept every NCAA National Player of the Year award as a freshman. The flags are modest run-jump athleticism (35" vertical) and positional size (6'8.25") for a franchise big - the highest floor in the class, ceiling capped mostly by role fit.

players.name: Caleb Wilson
players.scouting: Elite defensive tools for a forward - strong steal (2.8%) and block (4.4%) rates, plus productive rebounding on both ends (10.5% OREB, 22.2% DREB) and efficient rim scoring (57.8% FG). Season cut short at 24 games by thumb surgery. The swing skill is shooting: a 25.9% clip on low 3-point volume, offset by a merely functional 71.3% FT, leaves his perimeter jumper as the main development question.

players.name: Keaton Wagler
players.scouting: An efficient, high-feel combo guard - 59.6% true shooting at 25.7% usage with a strong 23.2% assist rate, clean turnover economy (7.5% TOV%, 2.48 A/TO), and a 9.4 BPM. Shot 39.7% from three and 79.6% from the line, both clean signals for his outside shot.

players.name: Mikel Brown Jr.
players.scouting: A dynamic scoring point guard - 18.2 PPG and a 30.3% assist rate at extremely high usage (31.0%), highlighted by a 45-point game that broke an ACC freshman scoring record. Turnover economy (11.8% TOV%) is more middling than dominant, and a modest overall BPM relative to his box-score volume, plus a back injury that limited him to 21 games, are the swing questions.

players.name: Darius Acuff Jr.
players.scouting: SEC Player of the Year and Rookie of the Year as a true freshman - 23.5 PPG and a 32.2% assist rate at high usage (29.5%), with elite touch (80.9% FT, 44% on ~6 3PA/game) and excellent turnover economy (6.5% TOV%, 3.1 A/TO). The flags are physical: a 6'3" frame with only a ~6'6" wingspan and weak defensive stocks (1.3% STL, 0.9% BLK) cap his defensive floor.

players.name: Kingston Flemings
players.scouting: A true lead guard - 16.1 PPG, 5.2 APG, and a strong 32.6% assist rate at high offensive efficiency (123.3 ORtg) with clean turnover economy (7.5% TOV%) and good stocks (3.0% STL). All-Big 12 First Team as a freshman. The physical profile is the concern: at 6'2.5" with a 6'3.5" wingspan, he had the shortest wingspan of any guard prospect evaluated at the combine, which strong athletic testing (40.5" max vertical) only partly offsets.

players.name: Morez Johnson Jr.
players.scouting: A hyper-efficient finisher and rebounder - 67.7% true shooting on modest usage (21.1%), with elite offensive (12.8%) and strong defensive (18.9%) rebounding rates, plus a real shot-blocking rate (4.8% BLK) for a play-finishing big. Helped Michigan to a national championship and earned All-Big Ten Defensive Team honors. A modest assist rate (8.4%) and minimal shot creation confirm a play-finisher/rim-runner profile rather than a shot-creator.

players.name: Brayden Burries
players.scouting: An efficient scoring guard on modest usage - 61.6% true shooting with a strong 3-point clip (39.1%) and excellent turnover economy (7.8% TOV%), plus a solid 2.8% steal rate. He's notably older than most of this class (turned 20 during the season) and his 14.2% assist rate is thin for a guard, pointing to more of an off-ball scoring role than lead creation at the next level.

players.name: Yaxel Lendeborg
players.scouting: Big Ten Player of the Year as a graduate transfer from UAB - elite efficiency at modest usage (64.6% TS on 20.5% USG), a 141.5 ORtg, and a well-rounded box score (15.1/6.8/3.2) that helped Michigan win the national title. Elite turnover economy (6.0% TOV%, 3.1 A/TO) and strong two-way impact (15.2 BPM) round out one of the best all-around profiles in this class. The obvious caveat is age: at roughly 23.4 on draft night, he's several years older than the freshmen atop this board, which tempers how much of this production should be expected to translate.

players.name: Aday Mara
players.scouting: Historic physical tools - a 7'6" wingspan and 9'9" standing reach, tied for the second-longest reach in combine history - paired with real passing feel for a center (19.0% AST%) and elite rim protection (12.0% BLK%, Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year). The high turnover rate (14.4% TOV) and modest 56.4% FT are the clearest flags; efficiency (65.8% TS, 67.3% eFG) is otherwise excellent.

players.name: Nate Ament
players.scouting: A high-usage (27.8%) wing scorer who started all 35 games, averaging 16.7/6.3/2.3 with 196 made free throws (3rd-most in Tennessee history) and a 7'1" wingspan that supports his defensive upside. Modest playmaking (14.8% AST%) and ball security (10.7% TOV%), plus real activity on defense (1.9% STL, 2.7% BLK). Efficiency (53.4% TS, 45.2% eFG) is still the clearest developmental question given his scoring role. Reached Milwaukee via the Miami pick from the Giannis Antetokounmpo trade.

players.name: Hannes Steinbach
players.scouting: Led all of Division I with 22 double-doubles as a true freshman and was the Big Ten's highest-scoring freshman (18.5 ppg), pairing plus efficiency (.636 TS%, 57.7% FG) with elite rebounding rates on both ends (25.0% DREB%, 14.3% OREB%). A quiet passer (9.1% AST%) for a high-usage big; how his handle and playmaking scale up will shape his offensive role next.

players.name: Dailyn Swain
players.scouting: Became the third SEC player in 30 years to average 17+ points, 7+ rebounds and 3+ assists on 50%+ shooting, in his lone season at Texas after transferring from Xavier - legitimate three-level scoring (54.2% FG, 34.4% 3P, 81.5% FT) with real passing feel for his size (21.3% AST%) and above-average defensive activity (2.8% STL%).

players.name: Bennett Stirtz
players.scouting: A 22-year-old grad transfer (Northwest Missouri State to Drake to Iowa) whose age is the central caveat on an otherwise loud statistical profile: 19.8 ppg on plus efficiency (.565 eFG%, 84.8% FT), elite turnover economy (7.4% TOV%), and a well-rounded line (4.4 apg, 24.9% AST%). The open question the box score can't answer is how much of this was an age/experience edge over younger Big Ten defenders.

players.name: Ebuka Okorie
players.scouting: Led the ACC in scoring as a true freshman (23.2 ppg, seventh nationally) on heavy usage (30.1%) with strong efficiency (.589 TS%) and clean turnover economy (10.4 TOV%) for a lead guard. Good stocks for a small guard (2.7% STL, 1.0% BLK) but undersized (6'1.25" barefoot, sub-8'2" reach) relative to the defensive workload a lead guard eventually draws.

players.name: Christian Anderson Jr.
players.scouting: Big 12 Most Improved Player and a third-team All-American as a sophomore, leading the conference in assists (7.4 apg, 35.2% AST% - elite for a guard) while scoring 18.5 a game on strong efficiency (.626 TS%, 41.5% on 3s, 80.5% FT). A 40.5" max vertical stands out; defensive stocks (2.2% STL, 0.7% BLK) are middling for his usage.

players.name: Allen Graves
players.scouting: A redshirt freshman who won WCC Freshman of the Year and Sixth Man of the Year: elite efficiency (.615 TS%, 29.6 PER), a rare all-around defensive/rebounding profile for a stretch big (4.9% STL, 5.0% BLK, 13.9% OREB - each at or near the 99th percentile), and the best turnover economy in this class (5.5% TOV%). Efficient on all three levels (51% FG, 41% 3P, 75% FT) with a 7'0" wingspan. The real caveat is competition level - a mid-major bench role, not high-major minutes.

players.name: Jayden Quaintance
players.scouting: Played just 4 games before a torn ACL/meniscus ended his season in February - by far the smallest sample in this class, so every rate stat above (all flagged small-sample) is closer to noise than signal. What's real: he arrived at Arizona State at 16 and played his entire underclass college career (Arizona State, then Kentucky) as a 17-year-old, and his measurements are exceptional for a modern anchor - a 7'4.5" wingspan and 9'1" reach on a 6'8.25" frame. The medical timeline, not the stat line, is the story here.

players.name: Karim López
players.scouting: A Mexican wing who played 2025-26 for the New Zealand Breakers (NBL Next Star pathway) rather than college, averaging 11.9 ppg/6.1 rpg/1.9 apg on 49% FG against a senior pro league. No KenPom/NCAA advanced-stat pipeline exists for his profile (DraftBallr also has no linked stat data for him), so this card leans almost entirely on measurables - 6'11.5" wingspan and 8'9.5" reach on a 6'8.25" frame - and per-game production.

players.name: Labaron Philon Jr.
players.scouting: The only D-I player to average 22.0 points and 5.0 assists on 50%+ shooting, with an elite 31.9% assist rate at high usage (29.9%) that marks him as a true offensive engine, and clean turnover economy for that role (8.8% TOV%, not the flag it first looked like). The real questions are defensive: modest stocks (2.0% STL, 0.5% BLK) and a negative DBPM for a 6'2.5" guard asked to check ball-handlers.

players.name: Zuby Ejiofor
players.scouting: Big East Player of the Year, Defensive Player of the Year, and Academic Player of the Year in the same season - the only high-major player in the country to lead his team and rank top-10 in his conference in scoring, rebounding, assists and blocks (16.3/7.3/3.5/2.2). Elite block rate (7.2%) and assist rate for a big (23.0%) stand out; plus efficiency (.609 TS%) and a 7'2" wingspan round out a well-rounded two-way profile.

players.name: Cameron Carr
players.scouting: Set Baylor's single-season scoring record for a sophomore (642 points, 18.9 ppg) on plus efficiency (61.8% TS, 57.6% eFG) and explosive tools - a 42.5" max vertical and a 7'0.75" wingspan on a 6'4.5" frame give him real defensive-playmaking upside (elite 3.9% block rate for a guard). Third-Team All-Big 12. Modest usage (24.3%) and assist rate (13.7%) suggest more off-ball scorer than lead ball-handler at the next level; he's also a thin 184 lbs.

players.name: Sergio de Larrea
players.scouting: Not a college prospect - plays professionally for Valencia Basket in Spain's ACB/EuroLeague, so this profile sits outside the NCAA pool it's being compared against. Named 2025-26 Liga Endesa Best Young Player in a limited ~18 min/game bench role (9.6/3.0/3.7), with clean touch (81.3% FT, 40.7% 3P) and real playmaking feel for his age. Advanced rate stats aren't tracked the same way in ACB (checked draftballr.com - profile exists but shows only placeholder zeros), so they're blank here rather than approximated.

players.name: Tarris Reed Jr.
players.scouting: Elite finishing efficiency (61.4% TS, nation-leading 60.7% FG) and rebounding (9.0 RPG, 14 double-doubles, 24.5% DREB) anchored UConn's Big East title run - First Team All-Big East and NCAA Tournament East Region Most Outstanding Player. An 8.4% block rate and 17.8% assist rate for a center are both plus signals. Excellent length at the combine (7'4.25" wingspan, 9'2" reach) offset by a modest 33" max vertical; a four-year college player entering the league already 22.

players.name: Chris Cenac Jr.
players.scouting: First Houston freshman to lead the team in rebounding since 2011-12 (7.9 RPG, 26.1% DREB, 13 double-digit rebounding games), with plus length (6'11", 7'4" wingspan). A low-usage, low-touch role as a freshman (9.5 PPG, 19.5% usage, 5.5% AST) means the offensive role and shot-creation projection are largely unproven.

players.name: Joshua Jefferson
players.scouting: First player in Big 12 history to reach 450+ points, 250+ rebounds, 100+ assists, 70+ steals, and 25+ blocks in a season - a rare five-way stat-stuffer at forward. The 27.7% assist rate and 3.1% steal rate are well above the norm for his size (off-position skills the research literature flags as underrated); shooting (34.5% 3P, 70.0% FT) is the swing skill. A four-year college player (also played at Saint Mary's) entering the league already 22.

players.name: Alex Karaban
players.scouting: Reliable, low-mistake 3-and-D wing (85.1% FT, 37.4% 3P, elite 7.0% TOV%) who started all 40 games in his fourth UConn season. Combine tools are average for the position (6'11" wingspan on a 6'6.75" frame) but his standing vertical improved notably from his 2024 combine reading. Low usage (17.6%) confirms a connector/complementary role rather than a shot-creator.

players.name: Koa Peat
players.scouting: Physical, high-usage freshman forward (14.1/5.6/2.6, 24.4% usage) with good size and length for the position (6'11.25" wingspan on a 6'7", 245 lb frame) and a strong 16.7% assist rate for a big. All-Big 12 Third Team and Big 12 All-Freshman Team. Free-throw shooting (62.3%) is the clearest swing skill.

players.name: Bruce Thornton
players.scouting: Career-best fourth season as Ohio State's floor general (19.9/5.1/3.9 on 66.5% TS / 40.0% 3P / 82.9% FT, elite 5.8% TOV% at real usage). Measured shorter than his college listing at the combine (6'0" barefoot, 6'5" wingspan), which caps his defensive positional versatility, but the scoring efficiency at high usage is real and translatable touch.

players.name: Richie Saunders
players.scouting: Efficient breakout scorer (63.2% TS, 59.3% eFG, 18.0 PPG, strong 3.1% STL) whose season ended early on a Feb. 15, 2026 ACL tear (25 games) - he did not test athletically at the combine because of it. At 24+ on draft night he is by a wide margin the oldest player in this pool, and the ACL recovery is a real medical variable on top of that age.

players.name: Isaiah Evans
players.scouting: Sharpshooting wing (86.0% FT, 36.1% 3P) in his second Duke season, Third Team All-ACC with a 32-point ACC Tournament outburst against Florida State. Good length for a slight frame (6'8.75" wingspan on 186 lbs) and a strong 35.5" combine vertical, but a low-usage role (21.8% usage, 8.4% AST) means he projects as a complementary shooter more than a primary offensive engine.

players.name: Meleek Thomas
players.scouting: Arkansas's No. 2 scoring option as a freshman (15.6 ppg), pairing plus 3-point shooting (41.6%) with elite free-throw touch (84.3%) and elite ball security (5.3% TOV% - 99th percentile). Named SEC All-Freshman Team. The flag is playmaking: a 13.2% assist rate is thin for a combo guard, pointing to more of a scoring than table-setting role early on.

players.name: Trevon Brazile
players.scouting: 2026 SEC Defensive Player of the Year with elite rim finishing (65.3% on 2s) and rebounding (20.6% DREB%), plus a rare shooting touch for a big (34.1% on 3s). Notably old for a prospect - a fifth-year senior (~23 at draft) - which the age-curve research this project leans on treats as a real caveat on ceiling, and his low usage (17.2%) means the offensive role at the next level is still an open question.

players.name: Baba Miller
players.scouting: First Cincinnati player since Oscar Robertson to lead the team in points, rebounds, and assists in the same season (13.0/10.3/3.7). An elite passing rate for a forward (23.3% AST%, 98th percentile) and strong two-way rebounding/rim-protection rates (27.7% DREB%, 4.2% BLK%) are the calling cards. The clear flag is perimeter shooting - just 19.2% on 3s.

players.name: Ryan Conwell
players.scouting: Louisville's go-to scorer as a senior (18.8 ppg, career-high 638 points) on heavy usage (27.5%) and 34.5% high-volume 3-point shooting. All-ACC Second Team. Playmaking (16.5% AST%) and defensive activity are secondary to the scoring at this point in his game.

players.name: Braden Smith
players.scouting: Two-time consensus first-team All-American, Big Ten Tournament MVP, and the NCAA's all-time career assist leader - a staggering 43.0% assist rate (98th percentile) headlines an elite table-setting profile (8.8 apg, 3.0% STL%). Shoots it too (36.2% 3PT, 82.5% FT). Oldest player in this batch (a four-year college senior) - the doc's own counter-evidence section notes older prospects with clearly translatable skills (his passing) still hit at a real rate, so this isn't a pure red flag.

players.name: Jack Kayil
players.scouting: Not a college prospect - played professionally for Alba Berlin in Germany's Bundesliga (won the 2025 title) at just 19 years old, averaging 12.3/2.7/3.5 in a limited ~21 min/game role. That's real production in a strong pro league at a young age, but it isn't directly comparable to the college-based percentile pool here. (Checked draftballr.com - confirmed not in their database, consistent with him being a non-NCAA player.)

players.name: Dillon Mitchell
players.scouting: Low-usage energy forward at St. John's after transferring from Texas - efficient (55.9% FG, 56.1% eFG%) on very low usage (15.9%, 2nd percentile), with a strong all-around activity profile: 18.8% AST% for a forward, a 3.0 AST/TO ratio, and plus rates on both boards and steals (2.6% STL%). Profiles as a rebounding/effort/connector piece rather than a primary offensive option.

players.name: Otega Oweh
players.scouting: Career-best senior season after transferring to Kentucky (18.6 ppg, plus career highs of 4.8 rpg/2.7 apg/1.8 spg), most points ever by a UK transfer in a season. All-SEC Second Team. Good activity marks (3.1% STL%, 9.0% TOV%) on heavy usage (27.1%); passing (16.3% AST%) is the weaker part of the offensive profile.

players.name: Ja'Kobi Gillespie
players.scouting: Set Tennessee's single-season steals record (79) while running the offense at a high level (18.4/5.4/2.1, 30.2% AST% at 25.7% usage) and powering an Elite Eight run. First-Team All-SEC, SEC Newcomer of the Year. Elite ball security for his usage (8.3% TOV%, 2.48 AST/TO) and disruptive defense (3.6% STL%, 87th percentile) round out a well-balanced lead-guard profile.

players.name: Tyler Bilodeau
players.scouting: Efficient stretch forward at UCLA - elite true shooting (65.6%, 97th percentile) and effective FG% (60.9%) on real usage (23.7%), plus a clean shooting-touch profile (87.3% FT, 46.4% 3PT albeit on modest volume). The flags are passing (7.4% AST%) and defensive activity (1.0% STL%, 20th percentile) - a clear scoring-specialist profile.

players.name: Maliq Brown
players.scouting: A pure low-usage defensive specialist: he set the NCAA single-season DBPM record (8.93, since 2010-11) as ACC Defensive Player of the Year and Sixth Man of the Year, ranking 3rd nationally in steal rate and 3rd in two-point percentage (76.5%). The flags are obvious from the box score alone: 4.9 PPG on a 62.9/16.7/50.0 shooting split means he offers almost nothing as a shooter or self-creator - his NBA case is 100% defense and finishing.

players.name: Emanuel Sharp
players.scouting: A career high-volume marksman: Houston's all-time leader in made and attempted 3-pointers, he shot 37% on 7+ attempts a game as a veteran while leading the Big 12 in free-throw percentage (89.1%) - the FT%/3-point-volume combo public draft research treats as the cleanest shooting translation signal. A thin 11.1% assist rate against 23.2% usage points to a shooting-first, not playmaking, offensive role.

players.name: Felix Okpara
players.scouting: A play-finisher and rim protector: 59.7% from the floor, team-leading blocks (1.5/gm) and 69 dunks (8th nationally), on a 6'10" frame with a plus-4in wingspan and top-5 combine standing reach (9'4"). A 7.0% block rate and thin 3.1% assist rate confirm a low-touch, vertical-activity-only offensive translation, not shot creation.

players.name: Tyler Nickel
players.scouting: A pure movement shooter in his third college stop (UNC, Virginia Tech, Vanderbilt): 40% on 7.6 three-point attempts a game with a career-high 8-make outburst, on 44.5% overall shooting. Not a shot creator or passer (6.9% AST%) - the value proposition is spacing, full stop - but efficient enough (61.7% TS) to post a solidly positive BPM anyway.

players.name: Tobi Lawal
players.scouting: An English-born forward (VCU, then Virginia Tech) who missed nine games to an ankle injury but averaged 12.3/8.5 with a reported 45.5-inch max vertical - one of the top verticals at the combine. Efficient inside (54.2% FG) but streaky range (25% 3P), missed time, and thin passing (3.9% AST%) are the caveats - and DraftBallr's model actually grades his season as a net-negative impact (-6.8 BPM), a notably weaker read than the box score alone suggests.

players.name: Bryce Hopkins
players.scouting: A cautionary tale on availability: after averaging 15.8/8.5/2.1 across three seasons split between Kentucky, Providence, and St. John's, a bone bruise limited him to only ~3 games in his 2025-26 St. John's season - on top of an ACL tear that ended his 2023-24 season early. The prior-season talent is real; the medical file is the whole story here, so no draft-year college stats are populated above (not in DraftBallr's database either, likely for the same reason).

players.name: Jaden Bradley
players.scouting: A four-year college player (Alabama, then Arizona) who broke out as a senior: Big 12 Player of the Year, Big 12 Tournament MOP, and a driver of Arizona's Final Four run on 46.3/39.4/80.9 shooting with a strong 23.2% assist rate and clean turnover economy (9.1% TOV%). Old for this class relative to the top of the draft - the age-curve research this page leans on would flag him as a lower-ceiling, more finished role/connector bet rather than a high-upside swing, though the well-rounded stat line (7.6 BPM) is a real positive.

players.name: Izaiyah Nelson
players.scouting: A do-everything forward (Arkansas State, then South Florida): 15.9/9.6/1.2 with 1.6 STL and 1.4 BLK a game on 56.1% shooting, 18 double-doubles, and a rare sweep of his conference's Player of the Year, Defensive Player of the Year, and Newcomer of the Year awards. Strong two-way rate stats (14.4% OREB%, 5.9% BLK%) support the counting stats. Almost no 3-point range yet (14.3%) - the developmental swing is whether a jumper arrives.

players.name: Henri Veesaar
players.scouting: A stretch big who transferred from Arizona to North Carolina and broke out immediately: 17.0/8.7/2.1 on a 61/43/62 shooting line, Co-MVP with Caleb Wilson, and one of a handful of players in D-I history with 30+ threes, 30+ blocks, and 60% shooting in the same season. A 43% 3-point mark on real volume plus rim activity (3.8% BLK%) is a rare combination if it holds at the next level, though his overall BPM (1.2) is more modest than the box score alone suggests.

players.name: Ugonna Onyenso
players.scouting: A pure specialist: after stops at Kentucky and Kansas State, he averaged only 6.5 PPG at Virginia but led the ACC (2nd nationally) in blocks per game (2.9) in just 18.6 minutes, then set an ACC Tournament record with 21 blocks in three games - 9 of them against Duke, 2nd-most ever in an ACC Tournament game. An extreme 17.4% block rate (among the highest on record, worth a grain of salt) backs up the counting stats. Almost no offensive role - this is close to a pure rim-protection bet.

players.name: Lajae Jones
players.scouting: A do-everything wing rebounder at 6'7" who took a long five-school path (Tarleton State, Barton, St. Bonaventure, Florida State) before landing in the second round. Modest scoring (12.7 ppg on .427/.325/.763 shooting) but solid rate production across rebounds, steals and blocks (roughly a steal and a block a game). Advanced metrics weren't publicly available for his lone Florida State season, so this profile leans on per-game rates and shooting splits alone.

players.name: Nick Martinelli
players.scouting: Led the Big Ten in scoring for a second straight year (23.0 ppg) on plus efficiency (.510/.417/.809 splits, 60.2% TS) and set Northwestern's single-season scoring record as a senior. Elite ball security (5.9% TOV%, 98th percentile) and real offensive impact (7.4 OBPM), though at 6'6.5" barefoot with a 6'10" wingspan he lacks great length for a forward, and his defensive stats (1.4% STL, 1.2% BLK, 0.1 DBPM) are thin - the translation question is shot creation and defensive role at a bigger, longer position in the NBA.

players.name: Vsevolod Ishchenko
players.scouting: A 21-year-old Russian wing named the VTB United League's Young Player of the Year in 2025-26, averaging 8.7 points, 4.5 rebounds, 2.1 assists and 1.3 steals in a bench role (roughly 23 minutes a game) for Lokomotiv Kuban. A reported 7'0" wingspan on a 6'3" frame is a notable length outlier if accurate. These are pro-league per-game averages, not college rate stats, and shooting splits/advanced metrics weren't confidently available.

players.name: Narcisse Ngoy
players.college: Poitiers Basket 86 (France Pro B, not NCAA)
players.scouting: A 7-foot French rim protector and Pro B (French second division) MVP, posting big per-minute rebounding and shot-blocking numbers (10.7 ppg, 11.7 rpg, 2.7 bpg per one confirming source) for Poitiers Basket 86. Selected by Atlanta and immediately traded to the Clippers in an unusual draft-and-stash situation - he has since announced he will instead play college basketball at Auburn next season rather than turn pro immediately, so this stat line is his last available season, not necessarily his next one. The archetype read is unambiguous regardless: an anchor big built around a reported 7'7" wingspan and 9'6"+ standing reach.

players.name: Jaron Pierre Jr.
players.scouting: A four-school scoring guard (Southern Miss, Wichita State, Jacksonville State, SMU) who put up 17.6 ppg on .462/.370/.758 shooting in his final college season, 7th in the ACC in scoring. A below-replacement -6.3 BPM despite decent raw efficiency (56.7% TS) is a real flag, not just a volume-scoring story: thin passing (11.1% AST%) for a lead guard and modest usage (23.3%) for his shot diet mean the box score reads better than the impact numbers do.

players.name: Trey Kaufman-Renn
players.scouting: A four-year Purdue interior scorer who improved his efficiency every season, finishing at 57.8% FG with 14.2 ppg, 8.3 rpg, and surprisingly good passing for a big (17.2% AST%, 95th percentile). But the confirmed advanced read is a real flag: a below-replacement -2.8 BPM, driven by a weak defensive activity profile (0.8% STL, 1.0% BLK - both bottom-decile) that a strong OBPM (5.6) doesn't offset. The clearest offensive flag is perimeter shooting - a 15.4% clip on 3s and a 27.2% FT rate (5th percentile) - so his NBA role likely hinges on scoring exclusively inside the arc.

players.name: Malique Lewis
players.college: South East Melbourne Phoenix (NBL, not NCAA)
players.scouting: A Trinidadian wing who spent two NBL seasons with South East Melbourne Phoenix as a 'Next Star' developmental signee, improving to 7.1 ppg, 4.3 rpg, 2.2 apg, 1.2 stl on a healthy .460/.390/.800 shooting split in his second season. Modest counting stats reflect a bench role in a senior pro league rather than a lack of tools - a reported 7'1" wingspan on a 6'8" frame stands out.
